review_text:
My first 1 star review goes to...the very deserving Circus Circus.  This review has everything to do with their employees and nothing to do with the amenities of the hotel and casino.

We all know how fun it is to relive your childhood upstairs where all the games and circus shows go down so my friends and I spent some time up there.  We sat down to play Circus Downs, the game where you roll a ball into holes to try to get your horse across the board, but there was a cheater amongst us.  He stole a ball..and used 2 to play.  The worker did nothing to stop him and even awarded that jerk a prize at the end.  Seriously?! Little kids are playing this game and you're going to cheat them from a fair game??  When I asked the employee to let us play another round, he just ignored me!  He didn't even apologize for the cheater or simply say no.  A friend and I decided to report this situation to security...which turned out to be no help AT ALL.  

Us: *tap* Excuse me.
Security biatch: Don't touch me.
Us: I need to report an incident. We were upstairs-
Security: *with LOTS of attitude* Upstairs? Upstairs where? Midway? 
Us: Um...upstairs where all the gam..
Security: Get to the point. What.Do.You.Want?
*Oh hellll no! At this point..I've had it up to HERE with her attitude!*
Us: If you're busy and you need to be somewhere, then I can talk to someone else.  Just point me to the direction of a manager.
Security: *throws back her jacket flap to reveal her "badge* Security.
Us: Tells her the story...blah blah 
Security: What? So this is about a teddy bear? 
Us: NO, this about ethics and the way the employee inappropriately handled the situation.
Security: How old are you?
Us:......Why does that matter?
Security: I have the right to ask.

WHAT A BEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEE!!! Really?! You're a security guard and you're going around riding on your high horse??  Are you even doing your job securing the premises? I wish I had gotten HER name so that I could report her!!

As you can guess, talking to the manager of midway was pointless.  He apologized for the "terrible predicament" we were in and how he understands "it sucks."  WHY ARE THEY TALKING TO US AS IF WE'RE 8 YEARS OLD?! 

adijgoiasjdgoiasdg CIRCUS CIRCUS YOU GUYS SUCK!!!
issue: Employee ignored cheating, allowed the cheater to win a prize, and failed to address the complaint or apologize
fixes: Deploy a real‑time incident capture workflow in ServiceNow (or Zendesk) with a mandatory “Cheating Detected” ticket type that auto‑escalates to a supervisor within 15 minutes and logs the timestamp for audit. | - Introduce a weekly “Fair Play Review” meeting where the floor manager uses a standardized Google Form checklist to verify every prize award; any deviation triggers an automatic corrective‑action task in Asana. | - Implement a post‑interaction micro‑learning module on Axonify that runs quarterly, requiring employees to pass a scenario‑based quiz on how to handle cheating and apologize professionally, with completion tracked and tied to performance bonuses. | - Install QR‑code feedback kiosks at all service points that instantly send a Slack alert to the team lead when a patron reports misconduct, ensuring immediate acknowledgment and resolution within 24 hours. | - Create a KPI dashboard in Power BI that monitors “Cheating Incidents per Month,” “Average Time to Acknowledge Complaint,” and “Apology Rate,” setting a target of ≤ 0 incidents and ≥ 95% apology compliance for monthly review.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the customer issue of ignored cheating and poor complaint handling. Each is actionable with clear steps and names specific tools (e.g., ServiceNow, Asana, Axonify, Slack, Power BI). Concreteness is maximal with named systems and measurable KPIs. However, feasibility is low: installing QR-code kiosks, deploying new workflows in ServiceNow/Zendesk, and integrating multiple tools across systems requires significant budget, IT coordination, and time—far beyond trivial or low effort. Thus, feasibility is capped at 2.

review_text:
My review pertains only to the Pet Boarding facilities.

Their "Hound Hotel" sounds/looks really good online, but in person it was a real let down.  The kennel is in a room off the parking garage, steps from the city sidewalk.  I was told it was by a security desk, but when we arrived, that whole tower was closed and unattended.  If somone were able to get into the room, they could get to your dog through the space at the top of the cage, even though the cage was locked.  The cameras are a nice feature, but are you gonna watch the dog 24/7?  Even if you saw someone doing something with your dog, it would take you 10 minutes to get there from the other tower.  We packed up and went to the Reno Plaza hotel, who allows pets in the room.

If you care for your pet, you would not use these facilities.
issue: The online description of the "Hound Hotel" is misleading and does not match the actual facility experience
fixes: Deploy a **content‑gap audit** using ContentKing’s daily website crawl to compare the live “Hound Hotel” page against the original brief, flagging any mismatched attributes and generating a remediation ticket in Jira. | - Integrate **Google Business Profile (GBP) API** with your CMS so every update to the hotel listing automatically syncs within 15 minutes, ensuring all external descriptions are kept current. | - Launch a **quarterly UX audit** in Optimizely Full Stack that runs A/B tests on key value propositions; if the variant deviates >10 % from the baseline conversion rate, trigger a change request in Confluence. | - Implement **dynamic product validation** via HubSpot Workflows that send a pre‑checklist survey to new guests before arrival, requiring ≥90 % agreement on amenities listed before the stay can be confirmed. | - Set up a **post‑stay NPS trigger** in Qualtrics that automatically flags any rating ≤6 and creates a ServiceNow ticket for a follow‑up review of the promotional copy within 24 hours.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue of misleading online descriptions for the Hound Hotel. Each is specific, uses real tools (ContentKing, Jira, GBP API, Optimizely, HubSpot, Qualtrics, ServiceNow), and includes measurable actions. The only deduction is in feasibility: while some tools are existing, integrating the GBP API with a CMS, setting up dynamic validation via HubSpot Workflows, and triggering cross-system actions (e.g., Qualtrics to ServiceNow) require non-trivial coordination, configuration, and likely developer time—making them moderate effort, not trivial. Thus, feasibility is 3, not 5.

rationale: All recommendations directly address the security risk of an unattended boarding tower with pets. Each specifies exact tools (e.g., Milestone XProtect, Zebra TC52, HID GoKey), measurable actions, and clear processes. The only deduction is in feasibility: while each recommendation is technically sound, deploying RFID integration with baggage systems, QR-code gates with Bluetooth readers, and new workforce shifts during off-peak hours requires significant coordination, training, and cost—justifying a 3, not a 5. No assumptions were made beyond what was written.

fixes: Add a locking mechanism on all side doors** – install latch‑activated slide bolts (e.g., Stanley 165‑series) that require a key‑coded master lock to open any door except the primary front entry, thereby preventing access through the top. | - **Replace the current mesh with certified anti‑escape material** – upgrade to 0.8 mm galvanized welded wire mesh meeting ASTM F1347 standards; the thicker gauge eliminates gaps that could allow dogs to climb out while still allowing visibility. | - **Install a ceiling‑mounted alarm sensor linked to a central monitoring station** – mount a vibration/pressure sensor (such as Honeywell VSM200) above the cage that triggers an SMS alert to staff if the cage is opened without unlocking the main door. | - **Create a quarterly inspection checklist in iAuditor** – assign a responsible person to complete a digital form each quarter that verifies proper lock placement, correct mesh installation, and sensor functionality, with automatic compliance reporting to management.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the safety hazard of top access in the cage. Each specifies tools (e.g., Stanley 165-series, Honeywell VSM200), processes (iAuditor checklist), and measurable standards (ASTM F1347). Actionability and concreteness are maximal. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to the need for new hardware (sensor, mesh replacement) and integration with a central monitoring station, which requires cross-department coordination and non-trivial setup, exceeding trivial or low-effort thresholds.

review_text:
Ahhh Circus Circus Reno, lets take a tour shall we?  Keep in mind we're not expecting much, this IS Reno, not Vegas.

Lets check in!  Go to the front desk and be greeted by the most somber and unpleasant reception desk staff I've encountered.  Pretty much everybody we talk to up there seem to hate their life and made sure to show it.  What a great vibe to start our trip!

Take a walk through the casino floor on the way to the room.  Take a look around.  The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Reception and front‑desk staff were unfriendly and displayed a negative attitude
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment analytics dashboard (e.g., Medallia Experience Management) on all reception screens to capture guest tone scores; set a daily threshold alert when scores fall below 85 % and trigger a corrective coaching ticket in Zendesk. | - Institute a weekly 20‑minute “Positive Language” micro‑learning module on Axonify, requiring ≥90 % quiz pass before staff can clock out, with completion tracked automatically by the LMS. | - Launch a quarterly mystery‑guest program through Secret Shopper Services, assigning a numeric friendliness score; tie the average score to individual KPI bonuses and publish results on a shared Power BI report. | - Implement a post‑interaction QR feedback kiosk at check‑in that logs CSAT/NPS ratings; configure Zapier to push any rating ≤6 into a dedicated ServiceNow incident queue for immediate manager follow‑up within 24 hours.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the theme of unfriendly front-desk staff with specific, measurable actions. Each specifies real tools (Medallia, Axonify, ServiceNow, Zapier, Power BI, Secret Shopper Services) and concrete metrics. Actions are clear and executable. Feasibility is 3 due to moderate effort: integrating multiple third-party tools (Medallia, Zapier, ServiceNow), launching a mystery guest program, and deploying QR kiosks requires coordination, budget, and time—beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Room furnishings were outdated, bedding and towels were dirty, and basic amenities like matches were missing
fixes: Deploy a weekly “Furniture Refresh” checklist in Asana that assigns each floor a rotating schedule to replace at least two dated items per room; track completion rates and tie them to quarterly KPI bonuses. | - Install RFID‑enabled linen tags integrated with the hotel’s PMS (e.g., Opera) so that housekeeping automatically logs each towel change and triggers a “dirty towel” alert if a tag is not scanned after three consecutive nights. | - Add a mandatory “Basic Amenities Kit” step in the housekeeping SOP, with a digital compliance form in ServiceNow that requires staff to confirm presence of matches, toothbrushes, etc.; non‑compliance generates a real‑time work order for replacement within 15 minutes. | - Launch a post‑stay mobile survey (via Qualtrics) asking guests to rate “Room Furnishings” on a 1‑5 scale; set a target of ≥90 % satisfaction and link any score below 3 to a corrective maintenance ticket in the existing CMMS (Fiix).
rationale: All recommendations directly address the specific issues: outdated furnishings, dirty linens, and missing amenities. Each specifies tools (Asana, Opera, ServiceNow, Qualtrics, Fiix), measurable actions, and clear triggers. The steps are executable with no ambiguity. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to reliance on RFID tags (high cost, integration complexity) and real-time work order triggers requiring system upgrades beyond existing tools.

issue: The tram to the Sky Tower was unreliable and often did not work
fixes: Deploy IoT‑enabled sensors on each tram car (e.g., Azure IoT Central) to monitor voltage, current draw, and door status in real time; configure automated alerts to the operations control room when any parameter deviates ±10 % from baseline thresholds. | - Integrate a predictive maintenance module into the existing CMMS (e.g., Fiix) that runs weekly data‑driven wear‑rate analyses on motor bearings and lubrication levels, automatically generating work orders when projected failure probability exceeds 5 %. | - Implement a daily pre‑shift inspection checklist in iAuditor with QR code scanning at the depot gate, requiring photo evidence of brake pads, electrical connectors, and battery health before trams are dispatched. | - Install a passenger‑facing digital signage board at the tram stop powered by Twilio Notify API that displays live service status (operational/under repair) and estimated wait times, reducing reliance on verbal announcements and improving reliability perception.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to tram reliability, explicitly concrete with real tools (Azure IoT Central, Fiix, iAuditor, Twilio Notify), and actionably detailed. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying IoT sensors across all trams, integrating predictive maintenance into CMMS, implementing QR-based checklists with photo evidence, and installing digital signage all require significant capital, cross-team coordination, and infrastructure changes—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

issue: The property had a presence of drug‑using individuals and felt unsafe in public areas
fixes: Deploy a mobile incident‑reporting app (e.g., iAuditor) linked to the security desk so guests can log suspicious activity instantly; set a KPI to reduce “drug‑related incidents” by 30% within 90 days. | - Install AI‑powered video analytics (such as Axis Camera Station with Deep Learning rules) on high‑traffic corridors to detect loitering patterns and trigger automatic alerts to security personnel via Slack integration. | - Launch a “SafeZone” patrol schedule using the Kronos Workforce Central platform, assigning two uniformed officers per shift to perform random room checks and walk‑throughs, with compliance tracked through daily audit logs. | - Contract a third‑party security firm that provides a dedicated Drug‑Interdiction Unit equipped with handheld drug‑detection kits, and measure effectiveness by targeting a zero tolerance rate for positive tests on premises. | - Place QR‑code entry gates at all public entrances that require guests to complete a digital safety screening form in the Property Management System (PMS), capturing ID verification and behavioral risk flags before granting access.
rationale: Relevance is 5: All recommendations directly address safety concerns tied to drug activity and perceived insecurity. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but some require interpretation (e.g., 'random room checks' lacks definition). Concreteness is 5: Each recommendation names specific tools (iAuditor, Axis, Kronos, PMS) and measurable outcomes. Feasibility is 2: The third-party Drug-Interdiction Unit and QR-code entry gates with behavioral risk flags are high-effort, expensive, and raise privacy and operational risks; not trivial or low-cost to implement.

review_text: Raquel, Brittney, & some middle eastern dude that works in the VIP downstairs....all terrible customer service. As a VIP member I should be receiving better care. They were all rude and unhelpful. I will never stay here again. I can not believe this service. It takes over an hour after talking to 4 different people and I still don't have a wifi connection after I paid for it? Not okay.
issue: Rude and unhelpful staff providing poor customer service, especially for VIP members
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment analysis dashboard (e.g., Medallia Insights) that flags any VIP interaction with a negative sentiment score ≥ 7; supervisors receive automatic alerts within 15 minutes and must log a corrective action in the ticketing system before the next shift begins. | - Launch a mandatory “VIP Service Certification” program using the Lessonly LMS, requiring all front‑line staff to complete a 45‑minute microlearning module on VIP expectations and role‑play two simulated VIP scenarios, with completion tracked and tied to quarterly performance bonuses. | - Integrate a post‑interaction NPS survey into the CRM (Salesforce) specifically for VIP customers, triggering an automated follow‑up email from a senior manager if the response falls below 80 points, and logging the case for root‑cause review within 24 hours. | - Install a call‑monitoring and quality‑scoring workflow in Talkdesk that records every VIP call, tags them with a “VIP Handling” rubric, and schedules weekly 20‑minute coaching huddles where the top‑scoring agent shares best practices while the lowest‑scoring agent receives targeted feedback.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause of rude/unhelpful VIP service with precise tools (Medallia, Lessonly, Salesforce, Talkdesk), measurable actions, and clear triggers. Each is actionable and concrete. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to requiring integration of multiple systems (CRM, call monitoring, LMS) and cross-functional coordination—moderate effort, not trivial. No assumptions made; all claims are grounded in the response.

issue: Paid Wi‑Fi not working after an hour and multiple contacts
fixes: Deploy a real‑time Wi‑Fi health dashboard (e.g., using the “Wi‑Fi Inspector” module in SolarWinds NPM) that monitors signal strength, connection uptime, and bandwidth per device; set automatic alerts when any metric drops below 80 % so engineers can intervene within 15 minutes. | - Create a dedicated “Wi‑Fi Support Escalation Queue” in Zendesk with a 30‑minute SLA, and configure a Slack‑based escalation channel that automatically notifies the on‑call network ops team whenever a ticket remains unresolved after two contact attempts. | - Implement a weekly automated audit script (Python + NetBox API) to compare paid‑Wi‑Fi subscription records against actual usage logs; flag any accounts where usage stops before the billing period ends and route those cases to a remediation workflow in Jira. | - Launch a self‑service Wi‑Fi troubleshooting portal powered by a curated FAQ Knowledge Base hosted on Confluence, and embed a QR code at each hotspot that directs guests to a one‑click “Reset Connection” button that resets the session token via the hotel’s Meraki MSN controller.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue of paid Wi-Fi failure after one hour and repeated contacts. Each specifies exact tools (SolarWinds, Zendesk, Python/NetBox, Confluence, Meraki), measurable actions, and clear triggers. Actionability and concreteness are maximal. Feasibility is reduced to 3 due to the need for API integration (NetBox), script development, and cross-system coordination—moderate effort, not trivial.

review_text:
I work in Reno Feb-March, for the last ....years, beds have not been upgraded for many years, the air quality is very bad, especially for one whose profession is a vocalist.  haven't tried the food here,  my room had a broken heater and had to be replaced.  All in all, Would never stay here, but it's provided by my employer, so that's the catch.  don't know how Reno will sustain these large hotel casinos with such poor entertainment.

I'm on the road about 6 months a year throughout North America and Europe, and about 95% of hotels have free wifi.  Circus Circus charges $10.00 per day for wifi, an added out of pocket expense for business travellers.  For one who must make many international calls, free wifi is a must for making travel reservations.  The only hotels charging for wifi these days are the big budget hotels, or downtown Reno.  Just Sayin', time to change Circus Circus.   Not happy paying for premium wireless, when I can't log on to internet banking or try to watch a news video of a dangerous high wind weather alert.  Just a note to all of you who like comfortable new beds,  I would go with El Dorado.  They have new flat screen TVs, but Circus Circus still has the 1990 model.  You'd think with all of that gambling revenue, they could upgrade some important things.  Hotel premium wireless is $13.00 per day,  download speeds are .31 MBPS,  You might be able to watch a 30 second video in 3 hours at that rate.  Unacceptable.  
On a positive note, some good live music in the Lounge.
issue: Beds are outdated and uncomfortable, not upgraded for years.
fixes: Launch a “Bed Refresh” project in Asana with a KPI‑driven timeline (e.g., replace 10 % of beds per month) and assign a cross‑functional team to track progress via a custom Google Sheet dashboard. | - Negotiate bulk purchases through the existing furniture vendor contract and set up a quarterly reorder schedule in Coupa, targeting a minimum of 150 new mattresses per quarter. | - Deploy a mobile QA checklist in iAuditor for front‑desk staff to rate bed comfort on a 1‑5 scale after guest checkout; aggregate results weekly and trigger a corrective‑action workflow when the average falls below 4.0. | - Integrate RFID asset tags into all bed frames and link them to the property management system (PMS) so maintenance alerts automatically appear when wear‑indicator readings exceed 80 %.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to outdated bed comfort, explicitly name real tools (Asana, Coupa, iAuditor, PMS, RFID), and are actionable with clear steps. Concreteness is maximal. However, feasibility is only a 2: RFID integration with PMS and wear-indicator thresholds require major hardware, software, and system integration—likely months and significant cost—making it high-effort and not trivial. This violates the strict feasibility threshold for a 5.

issue: Wi‑Fi costs extra, is slow (~0.31 Mbps), and inadequate for business needs.
fixes: Deploy a high‑density Wi‑Fi network (e.g., Cisco Meraki MR33) with automatic channel‑selection and QoS rules configured via the cloud portal, then run a speed‑test audit (using Ookla Speedtest API) on every floor for two weeks to verify ≥10 Mbps per user. | - Introduce a “Business‑Grade Wi‑Fi” tier in the billing engine (e.g., Stripe Billing) priced at $29/month, offering unlimited data and priority bandwidth, and automatically upgrade any account flagged by the usage analytics dashboard (Google Data Studio) as exceeding 500 MB/day upload traffic. | - Install a real‑time performance monitoring agent (e.g., Netscout Vantage) on all access points to trigger alerts when latency exceeds 50 ms or throughput falls below 5 Mbps, prompting immediate AP reboot or firmware update via automated scripts scheduled in Azure Automation. | - Launch a self‑service VPN solution built on OpenVPN Cloud, granting corporate users a dedicated tunnel with a minimum 20 Mbps guaranteed bandwidth, and promote it through a one‑click “Enable Business VPN” button in the existing guest portal.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the customer’s issue of slow, expensive, and inadequate Wi-Fi. Each is actionable with clear steps and specific tools (e.g., Cisco Meraki, Stripe Billing, OpenVPN Cloud). Concreteness is excellent with named tools, KPIs, and metrics. However, feasibility is low: deploying new hardware (Meraki MR33), integrating multiple third-party systems (Netscout Vantage, Azure Automation), and building a self-service VPN are high-effort, costly, and not trivial to implement—especially for a business with inadequate existing infrastructure. This exceeds 'low effort' and falls into 'high effort' territory.

review_text: My fiancé and I will no longer be valued customers as we were not treat with respect. Worst experience ever!!!
issue: Staff were disrespectful and did not treat us with respect
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment analysis add‑on (e.g., Clarabridge) in the ticketing platform that flags any interaction scoring below 80 % on respect metrics and automatically routes it to a supervisor for immediate follow‑up. | - Launch a mandatory micro‑learning series on professional conduct via the Lessonly LMS, requiring staff to complete a 15‑minute module and pass a short quiz every quarter, with completion tracked in the HRIS. | - Institute a “Respect KPI” measured by quarterly mystery‑shopper scores; tie 10 % of quarterly bonuses to achieving ≥ 90 % compliance, and publish results on an internal dashboard for transparent accountability. | - Integrate a weekly 20‑minute role‑play session into the team calendar using the PlayVox platform where agents rehearse de‑escalation scripts focused on respectful language, and record performance for manager review.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to disrespect issues, crystal clear in action steps, and name specific tools and metrics. However, feasibility is scored 2 because deploying Clarabridge, PlayVox, and integrating HRIS-linked microlearning with mandatory quizzes requires significant budget, cross-team coordination, and new software integration—far beyond trivial or low effort. review_text:
I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive. 

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: The motel is extremely cheap, suggesting low quality and poor value.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Value‑Add Audit” using the Pricefx pricing analytics platform to map every room rate against competitor data and guest‑perceived benefits; adjust rates where the price‑quality gap exceeds 10 % to restore perceived value. | - Launch a targeted “Premium Amenities” package (e.g., upgraded linens, complimentary Wi‑Fi, breakfast vouchers) priced at a 15 % premium over the base rate, tracked via the hotel’s PMS (e.g., Opera) to measure uptake and revenue impact. | - Introduce a transparent “Quality Commitment” seal on all marketing collateral and the website, backed by a post‑stay NPS survey linked to a $20 gift‑card incentive through Qualtrics, aiming to lift satisfaction scores above 80 %. | - Implement a tiered loyalty program in the GuestU mobile app that awards points redeemable for discounts or free upgrades, and monitor redemption rates weekly to ensure at least 20 % of guests participate.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Pricefx, Opera, Qualtrics, GuestU) and measurable actions. review_text:
I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive. 

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: Arcade games are outdated and overpriced, diminishing enjoyment.
fixes: Quarterly Game Refresh Cycle:** Use a procurement workflow in Coupa to onboard two new licensed titles per quarter, replacing at least half of the current arcade inventory with modern games, and track uptake via built‑in analytics dashboards. | - **Dynamic Pricing Engine:** Deploy a price‑optimization module (e.g., Pricefx) that automatically adjusts game prices based on real‑time play volume and competitor data, ensuring rates stay competitive while maintaining margin targets. | - **Augmented Reality Integration:** Install Samsung Galaxy Tab S8 kiosks running a custom AR game launcher developed in Unity, offering free demo levels and optional in‑game purchases tied to the existing loyalty program. | - **Gamification Rewards Program:** Launch a PlayPass mobile app where players earn points redeemable for discounts and exclusive content, measured by weekly sign‑up rate and average order value lift of ≥15%.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address outdated games and overpricing. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but assume existing integration capabilities. Concreteness is 5: specific tools (Coupa, Pricefx, Unity, Galaxy Tab S8) and metrics (uptake, sign-up rate, 15% lift) are named. Feasibility is 2: deploying AR kiosks, custom Unity app, and PlayPass app requires significant budget, cross-team coordination, and months of development—far beyond trivial or low effort.

review_text:
We made reservations just a day prior and then arrive only to be told that all circus performance acts are off until they finish construction of a new stage (which is due to open in Nov). This issue here is they made no mention of this on their webpage. When I complained they offered a full refund on the spot, but after driving all day we were not in the mood to shop around for another hotel.
Also, it was apparent the bedding either wasn't washed or not washed enough.
issue: Circus performance was cancelled without prior notice on the website, leading to disappointment.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time “Event Status” API (e.g., integrate with Eventbrite’s status endpoint) that automatically updates the public website within 5 minutes of any cancellation, ensuring visitors see current availability before booking. | - Configure Salesforce Service Cloud to trigger an automated email/SMS alert to ticketed guests the moment a show is canceled, using pre‑approved cancellation templates and a scheduled workflow rule. | - Establish a weekly “Show‑Ops Dashboard” in Power BI that pulls live data from the event scheduling system and flags any upcoming performances lacking confirmed venue contracts, prompting proactive rescheduling or refund offers. | - Implement a post‑cancel compensation workflow in Zendesk that issues a $10 voucher code and logs the incident; set a KPI to resolve all cancellations within 24 hours and review compliance monthly.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root cause of last-minute cancellations without notice. Each is highly actionable, specifies tools (Eventbrite API, Salesforce, Power BI, Zendesk), and includes measurable KPIs. However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: integrating Eventbrite’s API, building a real-time dashboard, and implementing cross-system workflows require significant engineering, coordination, and budget—far beyond trivial or low-effort changes.

review_text:
This review is on the kids Midway game area:

As a child, I lived for Circus Circus! My family would come here several times a year and it was amazing and fun.

I took my 9 year old son here this past weekend for the first time and it was garbage to how it was before. They did not at all upgrade this place. They even took out one of the popular games Fantasia! The shows they had going on werent that entertaining. The games were getting overpriced and there wasn't really any new and fun exciting games. My son basically got bored in an hour and really wasn't that entertained by everything. I had to agree with him big time because I knew it was trash compared to what it was before. They need to do a huge upgrade to this place! I'm sure if they did, they would get a lot more visitors.
issue: The kids Midway area has not been upgraded and feels outdated compared to its past condition
fixes: Deploy a **SpacePlanner + SketchUp** digital renovation workflow to map existing Midway layout, set design criteria (e.g., themed zones, age‑specific play areas), and generate concept boards within two weeks. | - Launch a **KPI‑driven upgrade sprint**: track weekly “upgrade completion” metrics in Asana, assign each task a owner, and aim for a 90 % finish rate by the end of Q3. | - Secure a **vendor contract with a certified amusement‑equipment supplier** (e.g., Chance Rides) to replace at least three aging rides per month, with delivery schedules managed via the supplier’s ERP portal. | - Introduce a **real‑time guest feedback kiosk** at the entrance of the Midway using Qualtrics QR codes; analyze responses weekly and prioritize improvements based on the top‑ranked “outdated appearance” complaints.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the outdated Midway issue, explicitly actionable with clear steps, and grounded in specific tools (e.g., SketchUp, Asana, Qualtrics, Chance Rides ERP). However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: securing a vendor contract with a certified supplier, launching a KPI-driven sprint with weekly tracking, and deploying real-time kiosks with QR codes all require significant coordination, budget, and infrastructure changes—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.
